The morning my husband stopped being a man and became a wound, the light was golden and the birds in the courtyard sang as if the omniverse had been made for that exact hour. I remember because I stood at the window and thought, *light should not be this indifferent*. Rogr sat on the edge of the bed behind me, not moving, not speaking, his presence a low hum of wrongness that had settled into our home ten years prior and had never once lifted.

I turned. His eyes were open, but they focused on a point that didn’t exist—a middle distance that I had learned to call the God-place. A thin rill of saliva traced the corner of his mouth. When I leaned to wipe it, his body didn’t register my touch. The muscles of his forearms, once heavy from his trade as a stellar cartographer, had dwindled into cords. He breathed, he blinked, occasionally he would emit a sound that was not a word but a soft, sustained note like a single violin string drawn tight and never released. According to the physicians, he was physically intact. According to the mind-healers, his psyche was an open wound that had cauterized around a shape too vast to be excised.

I was his wife, Rosa. And I loved him with a ferocity that had, over the past decade, turned itself into a slow-burning engine of desperation.

Before the audience, I had been proud. A delegate from our universe—the Elysian Concordance, a cluster of psionic hive-minds and energy-beings—Rogr had been selected because of his steady temperament, his eloquence, his faith in the new order. We had been subjugated, yes, but subjugation under Godric’s Thronegate had meant an end to war, an end to predation, an end to the thousand petty tyrannies that once devoured our worlds. I believed, as I still do, that the king’s rule is the best thing to ever happen to sentient life. My father had received the Awakening retroactively; he could now lift a mountain, but he used his strength to carve irrigation channels for the northern colonies. My brothers trained in disciplines of wisdom, and the streets were so safe that children played in the dark. Even the stars felt orderly, as if someone had finally bothered to hang them straight. So when Rogr left for the capital, I kissed him and said, “You’ll see—he’s not what they say. He’s a father, not a tyrant.”

Rogr returned three weeks later. He stepped through our door and his face was a porcelain mask, cracked across the cheek but still holding. That first night, he whispered fragments: the king’s *voice*, how it coiled under his skin; the king’s *displeasure*, a thing so small and so complete that it unmade something in his core. He couldn’t describe it further. The language failed him. But his body told the truth. He slept in shudders. He would wake screaming without sound. And within a week, he had retreated to the silent, staring place where he has lived ever since.

I started my attempts within days. The first was simple: I summoned a local physician-mage, a woman with a gentle aura and certificates of psychic suturing. She placed her palms on Rogr’s temples and closed her eyes. For three seconds, nothing. Then she convulsed. Her body arced backward, feet kicking the floor, her mouth stretching into a silent howl. When she surfaced, she was sobbing and saying, “There’s a wall—no, a *presence*—he saw something, and it’s *still there*, a fraction of a fraction of a fraction, and it’s looking back.” She fled and never practiced again.

In the second year, I arranged for the greatest mind-healer in our quadrant, a Vythian whose species had evolved pure consciousness. He entered Rogr’s room in a vessel of liquid light, and the light curdled the moment it brushed my husband’s aura. The healer’s consciousness-form inverted, folding inside out in a series of polychromatic spasms until it was a wad of screaming geometry. It took six of us to shove the vessel out of the house.

The attempts became my obsession. I reasoned that if the trauma was the king’s face, then perhaps I could inoculate Rogr by exposing him to a rendered image of Godric in controlled increments. I commissioned an artist who specialized in royal portraiture. The painting arrived, a magnificent depiction of our king in his salt-and-pepper majesty, warm paternal eyes, the slight smile he wears in official holo-captures. I hung it in the hallway where Rogr could see it from his bed. He didn’t scream. He simply stopped breathing. His skin perceptibly greyed, and a fine web of ruptured capillaries sprouted across his cheeks. I tore the painting down, but the damage had deepened: from that day, he could no longer tolerate any depiction of authority at all, not even minor civic insignias.

By the fifth year, I had tried sound therapy, memory erasers, metaphysical insulation fields, even an illegal synaptic firewall procured from a shadow vendor in the Outer Rings. The firewall was supposed to partition the traumatic memory so that it couldn’t trigger the rest of his neurology. The moment the firewall brushed against the God-place in his mind, it blue-screened across every sensor in the block and let out a burst of subsonic frequencies that shattered all the glassware in the kitchen. I held Rogr while he bled from his ears and I understood, really understood, that nothing I could do would ever touch this thing. The king’s displeasure, no matter how infinitesimal, had branded a scar that was not just in Rogr but in the underlying fabric of what he was. And that scar was still *amplifying*.

I am not a fool. I knew that Godric was the source. I also knew that Godric was the axis around which all of existence turned, and that without him our lives would return to chaos. So I did not hate him. I couldn’t. When the annual broadcasts of the king’s speeches played, I would weep at his kindness, his promises of protection, his genuine love for us. And then I would walk to the room where my husband lay rotting in mind and body, and the conflict would carve a new furrow in my chest. Pete, my son, grew up in that rift. He was eight when the audience happened, and eighteen now, a lanky boy with his father’s eyes and an unbendable devotion to the crown. He filled his room with scrolls of royal histories, practiced the Awakening disciplines with the fervor of an ascetic, and spoke of the day he would meet the king and thank him for our peaceful world. I never told him the full truth. How could I? He would have to reconcile that the same king had splintered his father into a living ghost.

By the tenth year, Rogr’s body began to fail in earnest. His muscles atrophied to the point where turning him required two people. His hair had whitened. His gums bled when I fed him broth. The physicians said he would not survive another year of this slow consumption, and part of me, the exhausted part, counted that as a mercy. Another part, the wife, renewed my search for a miracle. I stood in our courtyard under the alien stars of Thronegate’s core world and thought about Godric’s omniscience. He knew. He had always known. The thought that the king, who could dispel this with the merest flick of a thought, simply *believed Rogr could tough it out*, was a weight I couldn’t carry without screaming.

It was on an ordinary afternoon, with the light filtering amber through the dust, that the knock came.

I opened the door to find an old man with a white-streaked mutton chop, a face I had seen in historical texts. He wore the cobalt robes of the high court wizard, and his eyes were worn with a guilt that seemed almost physical. “Lady Rosa,” he said, and his voice had the roughness of a man who had spent his life talking directly to gods. “I am Blake. I believe I owe you an explanation, and perhaps a remedy.”

Blake. The king’s best friend. Thorin’s descendant, though nobody knew that but Godric. I let him in because my body moved before my mind could catch up. Pete, who was in the front hall polishing a ceremonial staff he’d bought with his apprentice wages, dropped everything and gaped. Blake saw him and a strange expression crossed his face—something like recognition, or maybe just the tired look of a man who saw sons everywhere. Then he turned to me and began to speak.

He told me everything. A joke. A prank. He had said to Godric, just before the delegates’ audience, “They think you’re a ruthless tyrant. Why not give them what they expect? Be the monster they imagine, just for a moment. It’ll be funny.” And Godric, who trusted him implicitly, had done exactly that. Not with cruelty—Godric was incapable of aiming cruelty at his subjects. But to *pretend* a mild displeasure, he had to reach into an aspect of himself that was normally throttled to zero, and even that fractional, held-back, almost-nothing expression of the king’s true magnitude had, for a mortal mind, been the equivalent of staring into the death of everything.

“He honestly thought it was harmless,” Blake said, his hands trembling. “He told me, ‘I held back. It was barely a twitch. They should be fine.’ Because he’s Godric, and his scale is broken. He didn’t understand. He never does, not really.”

I listened, and something cold and hard cracked inside me. Blake’s eyes were wet. “I only learned about your husband ten years later. Godric mentioned it casually, offhand, three days ago. He said, ‘There’s a delegate from the Year 28 intake, a man named Rogr, who still hasn’t recovered from that silly bit we did. I expected more resilience from mortals.’ I… I screamed at him, Rosa. I told him he’d broken a man’s soul and he was talking about it like a failed crop. And he looked at me—genuinely baffled—and said, ‘Then fix it, Blake.’ So I came here. To ask your permission.”

Pete, who had been listening with his mouth ajar, stepped forward. “Permission? To have the king heal him?” He was trembling with something that wasn’t quite anger, but close. “Why would you need our permission when his silence stole ten years?”

Blake looked at the floor. “Because I refuse to let the man I love as a friend operate on his whim alone. He won’t act on this unless you will it. I made him promise. I’m here to make this right, but I won’t drag you into his direct attention without your consent.”

I thought of Rogr’s whitening hair, his bleeding gums, the wall of screaming geometry, the physicians who came and went. I thought of the way my own love for the king had curdled at the edges but never soured fully. And I was terrified. Not of what would happen if Godric came—but of what it would mean if it was really this easy. If ten years of torture could be undone in an instant, then what was my suffering worth? What was I worth?

I walked to Rogr’s bedside. For the first time in years, his eyes shifted to mine when I called his name. “Rogr,” I said, “Blake is here. He can bring the king to heal you. Do you want this? Do you want to be free of it?” Something swam in the depths of his pupils, a tiny, alive thing fighting through a decade of static. His lips parted. A crackle emerged, and then a single word, ghost-thin: “Yes.”

Pete nodded. I nodded. Blake drew a shaky breath and said, “We consent.”

The air did not ripple. The light did not change. One moment the space beside the bed was empty, and the next it held a man of forty-five, with salt-and-pepper hair cropped short, a neatly trimmed beard framing a rugged, masculine face, and a casual tunic of unremarkable weave. He stood with the unassuming weight of a father who had just walked in from the garden. He did not glow. He did not radiate. And yet my entire body reacted: my knees softened, my throat constricted, a warm cascade of absolute safety and absolute terror poured down my spine because I knew, cellularly, that I was looking at something that was not in the same category of existence as me.

Godric’s omniscient gaze fell on Rogr, and a small, gentle frown touched his brow—the expression you might give a plant that had wilted because you’d forgotten it on the windowsill. “Ah,” he said, his voice resonant and ordinary. “I genuinely miscalculated the magnitude of the effect. I’m sorry, Rogr. You bore more than you should have.”

He did not gesture. There was no incantation, no weaving of cosmic energies. He simply *decided* that his subject should be healthy. The decision passed through the omniverse in a time slice smaller than I have words for, and Rogr’s body *snapped*. The atrophy reversed so fast it made a soft, wet sound. His cheeks filled, his skin clarified from jaundiced wax to a warm, living tan, his hair—still white but now lustrous—thickened, and his eyes, those dull marbles, ignited with a sharp, clear spark of presence. He sat up, entirely under his own power, and the movement was so fluid, so robust, that he looked like a man who had not only never been ill but had been crafted from some archetype of health itself. He coughed once, a bark of clear phlegm, and then gasped, “Rosa—?”

Ten years. Less than a heartbeat.

I was weeping before I knew it, and so was Pete, and we had him in our arms while the king stood back with his hands in his pockets, watching with the pleasant, slightly detached satisfaction of a craftsman who had set a bent hinge right. The joy in my chest was so huge it felt like a second heart, but beneath it, cold and dark, a wire of dread coiled tighter. Because I understood, in that moment, exactly how far beyond me all things were. My decade of sacrifice, my endless schemes, the parade of broken healers and shattered tools—none of it had been necessary. It had been the wasted motion of an insect throwing itself against a window while the owner of the house was simply… elsewhere. And the owner had returned and flicked the pane open without looking.

Blake cleared his throat, his own eyes red. “Godric, you should—”

But Godric was already speaking to me. “Lady Rosa,” he said, and his tone was so genuinely warm that I felt it like a blanket. “The compensation for this oversight is yours to name. I can grant you and your family governance over an entire universe of your choosing—its resources, its protection, the prosperity of a thousand generations. Or if you prefer something incorporeal, I can weave permanent joy into your lineage, or…”

I heard the words and understood them distantly. An entire universe. A thousand generations. It was an astronomical overcompensation that made the mind buckle. And yet I knew that to him it was akin to giving a street urchin a spare coin. I looked at Rogr, who was holding my hand with a grip that was almost too strong, his face streaming with tears but lit from within. He shook his head. I shook mine.

“Your Majesty,” I said, my voice cracking, “we don’t need compensation. We only wanted him back. You gave us that. That’s… that’s enough.”

Godric inclined his head, accepting it without argument, without pushing, and that acceptance was somehow more overwhelming than any insistence. He could have insisted. He could have simply rewritten our preference. But he didn’t, because in his strange, terrifying way, he respected our refusal as if it mattered. The dread coiled tighter.

Later, after we had shared wine and the strange, stumbling conversation that follows a miracle, Blake and Godric prepared to leave. I stood by the door, Rogr’s arm around my waist, Pete gawping at the king with the naked awe of a boy whose entire moral universe had just been validated. Godric turned to Blake and his eyes crinkled with a joke. “You see?” he said. “It worked out. Maybe next time we could stage a false assassination attempt during a diplomatic summit—you could pose as the assassin, I’d feign death, and we could watch the delegates sweat. That would be hilarious.”

Blake’s reaction was immediate and sharp. His face flushed a deep, angry red, and he jabbed a finger toward Godric’s chest with a familiarity that made my stomach drop. “I told you, no more pranks! Do you have any idea—any inkling—what a fraction of a fraction of your ‘mild displeasure’ does? You just saw it! I will end you, Godric, I swear on your own throne I will find a way to end you!” The words were shouted with the fury of a man who knew they were empty, and Godric laughed—a genuine, warm boom that made the glasses on the shelf tremble. “Blake, my friend, you’re magnificent when you’re angry.” And then they were gone.

I stood there, in the silence of my healed home, my healthy husband breathing beside me, my son still staring at the spot where the king had been, and I felt the most profound cognitive dissonance of my life. I loved Godric. I loved him for what he had done. I loved him for the safety he gave us, for the order he maintained, for the simple, impossible fact that he had chosen, without fanfare, to undo a wound that had defined my existence. And I was terrified of him in a way that had no outlet, because there was no enemy to fight. He was not our enemy. He was our king. The best king the omniverse had ever known. And he would never be anything but.

That night, Rogr held me in the dark, and I felt his strength—more than strength, a ridiculous, vibrant health that seemed to hum from his skin. He was alive, and we were together. But I also knew that somewhere out there, Godric was conceiving of another prank, another infinitesimal flex that might, by accident, destroy someone else’s soul. And there was nothing, absolutely nothing, I could do but live in his shadow and call it light.

Because that’s what it was. And that’s what it always would be.

Rogr bled from his ears for three days, and I learned something: whatever Godric had done, it wasn’t a memory. It was a fundamental rewrite of Rogr’s relation to existence, and it resisted everything I threw at it because everything I threw at it was *less real*.

I considered, once, a pilgrimage to the capital to beg the king himself for mercy. But the thought curdled in my stomach. I was a loyal subject, and I knew, with a certainty that bypassed logic, that approaching Godric with a problem like this would be like a bacterium asking a hurricane to stop blowing. It wasn’t that he would be cruel; it was that the very interaction might annihilate what was left of Rogr. Or perhaps I feared that he would flick Rogr’s health back on with the same casualness he had shattered it, and I would have to face the full scope of our irrelevance. So I didn’t go.

Pete, my son, grew up in that rift. He was eight when the audience happened, and eighteen now, a lanky boy with his father’s eyes and an unbendable devotion to the crown. He filled his room with scrolls of royal histories, practiced the Awakening disciplines with the fervor of an ascetic, and spoke of the day he would meet the king and thank him for our peaceful world. He never doubted. How could he? His world was safe. His mother’s love for the king was still there, a bruised but living thing. And so when he would come home and say, “Mama, the king’s latest decree expanded protection for inter-dimensional refugees,” I would smile and agree that it was wonderful, and then I would walk to the room where his father’s soul was a collapsed star and feel something in me crack further.

By the tenth year, Rogr’s body began to fail in earnest. His muscles atrophied to the point where turning him required two people. His hair had whitened, not with age but with a strange, brittle texture that made it snap under my brush. His gums bled when I fed him broth. The physicians said he would not survive another year of this slow consumption, and part of me, the exhausted part, counted that as a mercy. Another part, the wife, renewed my search for a miracle. I stood in our courtyard under the alien stars of Thronegate’s core world and thought about Godric’s omniscience. He knew. He had always known. The thought that the king, who could dispel this with the merest flick of a thought, simply *believed Rogr could tough it out* because of some ancient mortal resilience he remembered from his own humanity—it was a weight I couldn’t carry without screaming. And yet I didn’t hate him. I can’t explain that.

I was in the kitchen peeling a root vegetable when the knock came. It was an ordinary afternoon, light filtering amber through the dust, and I was crying without realizing it, tears dripping onto the cutting board. I wiped my face with a sleeve and answered. The man outside was old, with a white-streaked mutton chop adorning a face I had seen in historical texts. He wore the cobalt robes of the high court wizard, and his eyes were worn with a guilt that seemed almost physical—a man who had spent his life talking directly to gods and had somehow survived the conversations.

“Lady Rosa,” he said, his voice rough and low. “I am Blake. I believe I owe you an explanation, and perhaps a remedy.”

I knew the name. Every citizen knew Blake—the king’s trusted friend, the only mortal who could joke with Godric, the man whose descendants served as court wizards. I opened the door wider, and he stepped inside. Pete, who had been in the front hall polishing a ceremonial staff he’d bought with his apprentice wages, dropped everything and gaped. The staff clattered on the stone, and Blake caught it reflexively, a motion so fluid I remembered that this man was over seventy and still had the reflexes of a combat mage. He handed the staff back to Pete with a tired smile, and then turned to me.

He told me everything. A joke. A prank. He had been chatting with Godric just before the delegates’ audience. The room had been abuzz with tension; the delegates expected a tyrant, a bloody-handed conqueror, and Blake, in a moment of idle mischief, had said, “They think you’re a monster. Why not give them a show? Play the part, just for a few seconds. Let them sweat, and then you can reveal the good king underneath. It’ll be hilarious.” He hadn’t meant it maliciously. He’d assumed Godric would ham it up, maybe raise an eyebrow, use a stern tone. But Godric, who trusted Blake completely and had no context for “harmless prank,” had reached into a fraction of his nature that he normally kept buried under the absolute concealment. And that fraction—the infinitesimal, held-back, nearly-nothing expression of his displeasure—had been, for mortal minds, the equivalent of staring into the annihilation of all meaning.

“He honestly thought it was harmless,” Blake said, his hands trembling as he accepted a cup of water. “He told me, ‘I held back. It was barely a twitch. They should be fine.’ Because he’s Godric. His scale is broken. To him, the difference between a galactic empire and a grain of sand is negligible. He didn’t understand that a mortal psyche can’t brace against that kind of… truth.”

I listened, and something cold and hard cracked inside me. Blake’s eyes were wet. “I only learned about your husband ten years later. Godric mentioned it casually, offhand, three days ago. He said—and I’m quoting—‘There’s a delegate from the Year 28 intake, a man named Rogr, who still hasn’t recovered from that silly bit we did. I expected more resilience from mortals.’ He said it like one might mention a houseplant that needed more sun. And I… I screamed at him, Rosa. I told him he’d broken a man’s soul and he was talking about it like a failed crop. And he looked at me—genuinely baffled—and said, ‘Then fix it, Blake.’”

The court wizard—the legendary Blake—wiped his face with a sleeve and sat heavily on our bench. “So I came here. To ask your permission. I won’t let him act on this without your explicit consent, because it’s my fault. I gave him the idea. And I’ve learned, in forty years of standing beside that man, that the only check on his power is the people he loves getting angry at him. So I’m here, hat in hand, to beg you to let us make this right.”

Pete, who had been listening with his mouth ajar, stepped forward. His voice was a croak. “You’re saying the king… broke my father on *accident*? And then forgot?” The boy’s face warred between awe and horror. “But the king is good. The king is perfect.”

Blake looked at him with an expression of profound sorrow. “He is good. He is perfect for an omniverse. He’s a terrible, terrible judge of what a single person can survive. That’s why he needs people like us.” He turned to me. “Will you allow it?”

I walked to Rogr’s bedside. For the first time in years, his eyes—those dull marbles—shifted to mine when I called his name. Something had stirred inside him, perhaps the echo of Blake’s presence, or perhaps just the proximity of the only man who could lobby the king. “Rogr,” I said, my voice cracking, “Blake is here. He can bring the king to heal you. Do you want this? Do you want to be free of it?” Something swam in the depths of his pupils, a tiny, alive thing fighting through a decade of static. His lips parted. A crackle emerged, and then a single word, ghost-thin but undeniable: “Yes.”

Pete nodded, tears spilling. I nodded. Blake drew a shaky breath and said, “We consent.”

The air did not ripple. The light did not change. One moment the space beside the bed was empty, and the next it held a man of forty-five, with salt-and-pepper hair cropped short, a neatly trimmed beard framing a rugged, masculine face, and a casual tunic of unremarkable weave. He stood with the unassuming stance of a father who had just walked in from the garden. He did not glow. He did not radiate. And yet my entire body reacted: my knees softened, my throat constricted, a warm cascade of absolute safety and absolute terror poured down my spine because I knew, cellularly, that I was looking at something that was not in the same category of existence as me. The feeling was not of a lion among sheep, but of a shepherd who could, with a single thought, decide there would be no more sheep, or no more world for sheep to stand on, and then remake it all better.

Godric’s omniscient gaze fell on Rogr, and a small, gentle frown touched his brow—the expression you might give a plant that had wilted because you’d forgotten it on the windowsill. “Ah,” he said, his voice resonant and ordinary, lacking any echo or distortion. “I genuinely miscalculated the magnitude of the effect. I’m sorry, Rogr. You bore more than you should have.”

He did not gesture. There was no incantation, no weaving of cosmic energies. He simply *decided* that his subject should be healthy. The decision passed through the omniverse in a time slice smaller than I have words for, and Rogr’s body *snapped*. The atrophy reversed so fast it made a soft, wet sound—muscles knitting, bones densifying, skin flushing from waxen grey to a living tan. His hair, still white but now thick and lustrous, seemed to realign itself on his scalp. His eyes ignited with a sharp, clear spark of presence. He sat up, entirely under his own power, and the movement was so fluid, so robust, that he looked like a man who had not only never been ill but had been carved from an archetype of vitality. He coughed once, expelling a clot of dark phlegm that dissolved into nothing, and then gasped, “Rosa—?”

Ten years. Less than a heartbeat.

I was weeping before I knew it, and so was Pete, and we had him in our arms while the king stood back with his hands in his pockets, watching with the pleasant, slightly detached satisfaction of a craftsman who had set a bent hinge right. Rogr’s hands were warm and strong on my back. He smelled of health—of salt and clean skin, not the sour, closed air he had exuded for a decade. The joy in my chest was so huge it felt like a second heart, but beneath it, cold and dark, a wire of dread coiled tighter. Because I understood, in that moment, exactly how far beyond me all things were. My decade of sacrifice, my parade of broken healers and shattered tools—none of it had been necessary. It had been the wasted motion of an insect throwing itself against a window while the owner of the house was simply… elsewhere. And the owner had returned and flicked the pane open without looking. The sheer, casual ease of it undid something in me. I had screamed, I had wept, I had bargained with the fabric of reality, and this man had done it with a *decision*.

Blake cleared his throat, his own eyes red, moisture tracing the lines of his face. “Godric, you should offer compensation,” he said, his voice pointed.

Godric’s attention turned to me, and his expression was so genuinely warm, so open, that it cut. “Lady Rosa,” he said, “the compensation for this oversight is yours to name. I can grant you and your family governance over an entire universe of your choosing—its resources, its protection, the prosperity of a thousand generations. Or if you prefer something incorporeal, I can weave permanent joy into your lineage, or guarantee that your descendants shall never know a moment’s despair. Anything you can conceive, I can give.”

I heard the words and understood them distantly. An entire universe. A thousand generations. Permanent joy. It was an astronomical overcompensation that made the mind buckle. And yet I knew that to him, it was the equivalent of offering a street urchin a spare coin. His face showed no guile, no condescension—just a simple willingness to make things right in a currency he understood. I looked at Rogr, who was holding my hand with a grip that was almost too strong, his face streaming with tears but lit from within. He shook his head. I shook mine.

“Your Majesty,” I said, my voice a raw thing, “we don’t need compensation. We only wanted him back. You gave us that. That’s… that’s enough.”

Godric inclined his head, accepting it without argument, without pushing, and that acceptance was somehow more overwhelming than any insistence. He could have insisted. He could have simply rewritten our preference without our knowledge. But he didn’t, because in his strange, terrifying way, he respected our refusal as if it actually meant something. The dread coiled tighter, because it meant his kindness was real, and his power was real, and the two coexisted without friction. It meant he was not a tyrant in the way the delegates had feared; he was something infinitely more frightening: a good man who could crush you by accident and then mend the damage with a thought, all without ever intending harm.

Later, after we had shared wine—Rogr drinking with an ease that made me want to laugh and cry—Blake and Godric prepared to leave. I stood by the door, Rogr’s arm around my waist, Pete gawping at the king with the naked awe of a boy whose entire moral universe had just been validated. His hero had come, his hero had healed his father, and the world was once again a place where kings were good and sons were safe. I couldn’t bring myself to ruin that.

Godric turned to Blake and his eyes crinkled with the same mischievous light that I’d seen in my own son’s when he was building a toy catapult. “You see?” he said, his voice relaxed. “It all worked out. The prank was a success in the end—well, perhaps a bit delayed. But think of the possibilities, Blake. Next time we host a diplomatic summit, we could stage a false assassination attempt. You could dress as the assassin, I’d feign a dramatic death, and we could watch the delegates scramble. Their faces would be priceless. We could even have you dramatically unmask as the court wizard.”

Blake’s reaction was immediate and violently sharp. His face flushed a deep, angry red, and he jabbed a finger toward Godric’s chest with a familiarity that stopped my heart. “I told you, no more pranks! Do you have any idea—any *inkling*—what a fraction of a fraction of your ‘mild displeasure’ did to this family? And you want to fake an assassination?! I will end you, Godric, I swear on all the stars I will find a way to end you!” The words were shouted with the fury of a man who knew they were empty, who knew he was shouting at the omnipotent axis of creation, and who did it anyway because he was the only person alive who could.

Godric laughed. It was not a cruel laugh. It was a genuine, warm, delighted boom that made the wine glasses on the shelf tremble and created a brief, baffling sense of normalcy. “Blake, my friend, you’re magnificent when you’re angry.” He clapped a hand on his friend’s shoulder, and then the two of them were simply not there anymore.

I stood in the doorway, my healthy husband alive against me, my son babbling excitedly about how the king had smiled at him, and I felt the most profound cognitive dissonance of my life. I loved Godric. I loved him for the peace, for the order, for the simple, impossible fact that he had chosen, without fanfare, to undo a wound that had defined my existence. And I was terrified of him in a way that had no outlet, because there was no enemy to battle. He was not our enemy. He was our king. The best king the omniverse had ever known. And he would never be anything but.

That night, Rogr held me in the dark, and I felt his strength—more than strength, a ridiculous, vibrant health that seemed to hum from his skin, a vitality that would probably let him live another hundred years without trying. He was alive, and we were together. But I also knew that somewhere in the endless halls of the capital, Godric was gleefully planning another prank, another infinitesimal flex that might, by accident, shatter another soul. And there was nothing, absolutely nothing, I could do but live in his shadow and call it light.

Because it was light. And it was a light that would never stop burning, even when it scorched the very things it meant to warm.

The next morning, Pete went to his apprenticeship humming a royal anthem. Rogr made breakfast—eggs, the way he used to, seasoned with a spice from our homeworld that I hadn’t smelled in ten years. And I smiled, and I ate, and I felt the wire of dread coiled around my spine tighten another turn, a permanent, loving companion.

Because the king was good, and we were his, and there was no escaping that paradox. Not ever.

# Rosa

The morning my husband stopped being a man and became a wound, the light was golden and the birds in the courtyard sang as if the omniverse had been made for that exact hour. I remember because I stood at the window and thought, *light should not be this indifferent*. Behind me, Rogr sat on the edge of the bed. He did not move. He did not speak. His presence had become a low hum of wrongness that had settled into our home ten years prior and had never once lifted, a vibration you felt in your teeth rather than your ears.

I turned. His eyes were open, but they focused on a point that didn’t exist—a middle distance I had learned to call the God-place. A thin rill of saliva traced the corner of his mouth. When I leaned to wipe it, his body didn’t register my touch. The muscles of his forearms, once heavy from his trade as a stellar cartographer, had dwindled into cords that felt like bundled twine beneath the skin. He breathed, he blinked, occasionally he would emit a sound that was not a word but a soft, sustained note like a single violin string drawn tight and never released. According to the dozen physicians I had dragged through this house, he was physically intact. According to the mind-healers who had touched his psyche, the inside of him was a wound that had cauterized around a shape too vast to be excised.

I was his wife, Rosa. And I loved him with a ferocity that had, over the past decade, turned itself into a slow-burning engine of desperation.

Before the audience, I had been proud. We came from the Elysian Concordance, a swarm of psionic hive-minds and energy-forms that had been subjugated forty years prior, early in Godric’s expansion. But subjugation under Thronegate had meant an end to war, an end to predation, an end to the thousand petty tyrannies that once devoured our worlds. I believed, as I still do, that the king’s rule is the best thing to ever happen to sentient life. My father had received the Awakening retroactively; he could now lift a mountain, but he used his strength to carve irrigation channels for the northern colonies. My brothers trained in disciplines of wisdom so profound they could settle border disputes with a single conversation. The streets were so safe that children played in the dark, and my daughter—if I’d had one—could walk alone at midnight and never fear. Even the stars felt orderly, as if someone had finally bothered to hang them straight. So when Rogr was selected as a delegate to the capital—an emissary to thank the king for our new peace—I kissed him and said, “You’ll see. He’s not what the histories say. He’s a father, not a tyrant.”

Rogr returned three weeks later. He stepped through our door and his face was a porcelain mask, cracked across the cheek but still holding. I remember the way he flinched when the door clicked shut, the way his eyes tracked movement that wasn’t there. That first night, as I held him in our bed, he whispered fragments against my shoulder. His voice was dry paper.

“It wasn’t anger. It wasn’t even… Rosa, it was the opposite of anger. He was so calm. He stood there, and I couldn’t feel my lungs because the air around him wasn’t air anymore, it was a statement. And then he looked at me. Not at all of us—at me. And he was displeased. Just a little. A flicker. Like noticing a single speck on an otherwise flawless table. And I realized, in that flicker, that everything I was—my thoughts, my memories, my love for you—was that speck. And the only reason I continued to exist was that the speck wasn’t worth the effort of wiping away. He held back. Gods, he held back. But even the afterimage of the afterimage of his displeasure made me understand that I am not a real thing to him. I am a permission he grants.”

He couldn’t finish. He tried. Over the following days, he would open his mouth and a sound would emerge that was the shape of a word but no substance. The physicians later told me that the neural pathways related to his sense of self had been physically altered, as if a metaphysical pressure had compressed them into a tangle. His brain worked, but the part that told him he mattered was gone. He was a man who had looked into the face of his creator and found himself an accident.

I found out later that she had developed an irrational terror of mirrors and now lives in a windowless hospice.

In the second year, I arranged for the greatest mind-healer in our quadrant, a Vythian whose species had evolved pure consciousness. He entered Rogr’s room in a vessel of liquid light, and the light curdled the moment it brushed my husband’s aura. The healer’s consciousness-form inverted, folding inside out in a series of polychromatic spasms until it was a wad of screaming geometry. It took six of us to shove the vessel out of the house. The healer survived, but he tells everyone who will listen that the Thronegate peace is a lie, and now he lives as a paranoid wanderer.

The failure shaped me. I reasoned that if the trauma was the king’s face, perhaps I could inoculate Rogr by exposing him to a rendered image of Godric in controlled increments. I commissioned an artist who specialized in royal portraiture. The painting arrived, a magnificent depiction of our king in his salt-and-pepper majesty, warm paternal eyes, the slight smile he wears in official holo-captures. I hung it in the hallway where Rogr could see it from his bed. He didn’t scream. He simply stopped breathing. His skin perceptibly greyed, and a fine web of ruptured capillaries sprouted across his cheeks. I tore the painting down, but the damage had deepened: from that day, he could no longer tolerate even minor civic insignias. A simple seal of the throne on a tax document would make his pulse flutter like a dying bird’s.

It listened to my plea and agreed to excise the trauma in exchange for a sliver of my own soul. I agreed. The voidweaver unspooled itself into Rogr’s mindscape and promptly detonated. The detonation was silent, but I felt it in my chest—a cold implosion that left a scent of burnt nothing in the air. The voidweaver’s patron sent me a message the next day: *We do not trespass on the King’s echo. Consider your debt forgiven, and never contact us again.*

And yet I didn’t hate him. I can’t explain that. When the annual broadcasts of the king’s speeches played, I would still weep at his kindness, his promises of protection, his genuine love for us. And then I would dry my eyes and spoon-feed my husband.

And I’ve learned, in forty years of standing beside that man, that the only check on his power is the people he loves getting angry at him. So I’m here, hat in hand, to beg you to let us make this right.”

Pete, who had been listening with his mouth ajar, stepped forward. His voice was a croak. “You’re saying the king… broke my father on *accident*? And then forgot?” The boy’s face warred between awe and horror. “But the king is good. The king is perfect.”

Blake looked at him with an expression of profound sorrow. “He is good. He is perfect for an omniverse. He’s a terrible, terrible judge of what a single person can survive. That’s why he needs people like us.” He turned to me. “Will you allow it?”

I walked to Rogr’s bedside. For the first time in years, his eyes—those dull marbles—shifted to mine when I called his name. Something had stirred inside him, perhaps the echo of Blake’s presence, or perhaps just the proximity of the only man who could lobby the king. “Rogr,” I said, my voice cracking, “Blake is here. He can bring the king to heal you. Do you want this? Do you want to be free of it?” Something swam in the depths of his pupils, a tiny, alive thing fighting through a decade of static. His lips parted. A crackle emerged, and then a single word, ghost-thin but undeniable: “Yes.”

Pete nodded, tears spilling. I nodded. Blake drew a shaky breath and said, “We consent.”

The air did not ripple. The light did not change. One moment the space beside the bed was empty, and the next it held a man of forty-five, with salt-and-pepper hair cropped short, a neatly trimmed beard framing a rugged, masculine face, and a casual tunic of unremarkable weave. He stood with the unassuming stance of a father who had just walked in from the garden. He did not glow. He did not radiate. And yet my entire body reacted: my knees softened, my throat constricted, a warm cascade of absolute safety and absolute terror poured down my spine because I knew, cellularly, that I was looking at something that was not in the same category of existence as me. The feeling was not of a lion among sheep, but of a shepherd who could, with a single thought, decide there would be no more sheep, or no more world for sheep to stand on, and then remake it all better.

Godric’s omniscient gaze fell on Rogr, and a small, gentle frown touched his brow—the expression you might give a plant that had wilted because you’d forgotten it on the windowsill. “Ah,” he said, his voice resonant and ordinary, lacking any echo or distortion. “I genuinely miscalculated the magnitude of the effect. I’m sorry, Rogr. You bore more than you should have.”

He did not gesture. There was no incantation, no weaving of cosmic energies. He simply *decided* that his subject should be healthy. The decision passed through the omniverse in a time slice smaller than I have words for, and Rogr’s body *snapped*. The atrophy reversed so fast it made a soft, wet sound—muscles knitting, bones densifying, skin flushing from waxen grey to a living tan. His hair, still white but now thick and lustrous, seemed to realign itself on his scalp. His eyes ignited with a sharp, clear spark of presence. He sat up, entirely under his own power, and the movement was so fluid, so robust, that he looked like a man who had not only never been ill but had been carved from an archetype of vitality. He coughed once, expelling a clot of dark phlegm that dissolved into nothing, and then gasped, “Rosa—?”

Ten years. Less than a heartbeat.

I was weeping before I knew it, and so was Pete, and we had him in our arms while the king stood back with his hands in his pockets, watching with the pleasant, slightly detached satisfaction of a craftsman who had set a bent hinge right. Rogr’s hands were warm and strong on my back. He smelled of health—of salt and clean skin, not the sour, closed air he had exuded for a decade. The joy in my chest was so huge it felt like a second heart, but beneath it, cold and dark, a wire of dread coiled tighter. Because I understood, in that moment, exactly how far beyond me all things were. My decade of sacrifice, my parade of broken healers and shattered tools—none of it had been necessary. It had been the wasted motion of an insect throwing itself against a window while the owner of the house was simply elsewhere. And the owner had returned and flicked the pane open without looking. The sheer, casual ease of it undid something in me. I had screamed, I had wept, I had bargained with the fabric of reality, and this man had done it with a *decision*.

Blake cleared his throat, his own eyes red, moisture tracing the lines of his face. “Godric, you should offer compensation,” he said, his voice pointed.

Godric’s attention turned to me, and his expression was so genuinely warm, so open, that it cut. “Lady Rosa,” he said, “the compensation for this oversight is yours to name. I can grant you and your family governance over an entire universe of your choosing—its resources, its protection, the prosperity of a thousand generations. Or if you prefer something incorporeal, I can weave permanent joy into your lineage, or guarantee that your descendants shall never know a moment’s despair. Anything you can conceive, I can give.”

I heard the words and understood them distantly. An entire universe. A thousand generations. Permanent joy. It was an astronomical overcompensation that made the mind buckle. And yet I knew that to him, it was the equivalent of offering a street urchin a spare coin. His face showed no guile, no condescension—just a simple willingness to make things right in a currency he understood. I looked at Rogr, who was holding my hand with a grip that was almost too strong, his face streaming with tears but lit from within. He shook his head. I shook mine.

“Your Majesty,” I said, my voice a raw thing, “we don’t need compensation. We only wanted him back. You gave us that. That’s… that’s enough.”

Godric inclined his head, accepting it without argument, without pushing, and that acceptance was somehow more overwhelming than any insistence. He could have insisted. He could have simply rewritten our preference without our knowledge. But he didn’t, because in his strange, terrifying way, he respected our refusal as if it actually meant something. The dread coiled tighter, because it meant his kindness was real, and his power was real, and the two coexisted without friction. It meant he was not a tyrant in the way the delegates had feared; he was something infinitely more frightening: a good man who could crush you by accident and then mend the damage with a thought, all without ever intending harm.

Later, after we had shared wine—Rogr drinking with an ease that made me want to laugh and cry—Blake and Godric prepared to leave. I stood by the door, Rogr’s arm around my waist, Pete gawping at the king with the naked awe of a boy whose entire moral universe had just been validated. His hero had come, his hero had healed his father, and the world was once again a place where kings were good and sons were safe. I couldn’t bring myself to ruin that.

Godric turned to Blake, and his eyes crinkled with the same mischievous light that I’d seen in my own son’s when he was building a toy catapult. “You see?” he said, his voice relaxed. “It all worked out. The prank was a success in the end—well, perhaps a bit delayed. But think of the possibilities, Blake. Next time we host a diplomatic summit, we could stage a false assassination attempt. You could dress as the assassin, I’d feign a dramatic death, and we could watch the delegates scramble. Their faces would be priceless. We could even have you dramatically unmask as the court wizard.”

Blake’s reaction was immediate and violently sharp. His face flushed a deep, angry red, and he jabbed a finger toward Godric’s chest with a familiarity that stopped my heart. “I told you, no more pranks! Do you have any idea—any *inkling*—what a fraction of a fraction of your ‘mild displeasure’ did to this family? And you want to fake an assassination?! I will end you, Godric, I swear on all the stars I will find a way to end you!” The words were shouted with the fury of a man who knew they were empty, who knew he was shouting at the omnipotent axis of creation, and who did it anyway because he was the only person alive who could.

Godric laughed. It was not a cruel laugh. It was a genuine, warm, delighted boom that made the wine glasses on the shelf tremble and created a brief, baffling sense of normalcy. “Blake, my friend, you’re magnificent when you’re angry.” He clapped a hand on his friend’s shoulder, and then the two of them were simply not there anymore.

I stood in the doorway, my healthy husband alive against me, my son babbling excitedly about how the king had smiled at him, and I felt the most profound cognitive dissonance of my life. I loved Godric. I loved him for the peace, for the order, for the simple, impossible fact that he had chosen, without fanfare, to undo a wound that had defined my existence. And I was terrified of him in a way that had no outlet, because there was no enemy to battle. He was not our enemy. He was our king. The best king the omniverse had ever known. And he would never be anything but.

That night, Rogr held me in the dark, and I felt his strength—more than strength, a ridiculous, vibrant health that seemed to hum from his skin, a vitality that would probably let him live another hundred years without trying. He was alive, and we were together. But I also knew that somewhere in the endless halls of the capital, Godric was gleefully planning another prank, another infinitesimal flex that might, by accident, shatter another soul. And there was nothing, absolutely nothing, I could do but live in his shadow and call it light.

Because it was light. And it was a light that would never stop burning, even when it scorched the very things it meant to warm.

The next morning, Pete went to his apprenticeship humming a royal anthem. Rogr made breakfast—eggs, the way he used to, seasoned with a spice from our homeworld that I hadn’t smelled in ten years. And I smiled, and I ate, and I felt the wire of dread coiled around my spine tighten another turn, a permanent, loving companion. Because the king was good, and we were his, and there was no escaping that paradox. Not ever.

8. Godric looks disappointed. He turns to the air and says, "Gent, you were supposed to hold back. Why didn't you nerf yourself to the level I asked? They didn't even make you try. I need to teach you restraint." Tim sees the irony and wants to laugh/cry. Godric then says, "I'll show you how to hold back properly next time."

9. Tim is in a state of dread. Then Gent visibly shimmers, and a new layer of power blooms: Gent now has the ability to "author" the deaths of any threat before it even conceptualizes resistance—no, we can't do that (pre-annihilation). Instead, Gent evolves the ability to "echo-lock": it can create a perfect temporal loop of the final moments of any slain foe, and project it across all space and time as a warning, essentially turning the agony into a permanent broadcast that any potential rebel can witness. This is a new passive ability. Godric notices and his face lights up: "Oh, look! Gent learned a new trick. It's like a little puppy rolling over." He pats the air. Tim is horrified.

10. Tim thinks about the implications: Godric's amplification means that every Planck second, he's exponentially more powerful, so any future resistance has even less chance. The gap is not a gap but an expanding abyss. Tim's own magic, though amplified daily, is nothing. He feels his mind fracturing.

11. Godric then suggests they get lunch, and Tim can only nod. The story ends with Tim walking behind Godric, the weight of the omniverse pressing down.

We need to ensure that Godric's cruelty to opposition is shown, but he's genuinely kind to Tim and his subjects. So Godric's tone with Tim is friendly, maybe even joking. Tim's sarcasm might show, but in this situation it's muted by horror.

-- Start of story --

The morning the omniverse ended, I was eating a pastry. A flaky spiraled thing filled with sweet bean paste from the ninth district, where the bakers had mastered a recipe involving condensed starlight and something they refused to name. I had it halfway to my mouth when the King’s voice cut through the quiet of the Grand Observatory like a knife through butter.

“Tim,” he said, and his tone had that particular lightness it always carried when he was about to ruin my entire conception of existence. “Today’s the day. They’re finally here.”

I stopped chewing. Swallowed. The pastry sat on my tongue like a lump of cold ash. “Your Majesty, when you say ‘they,’ I assume you don’t mean the seasonal migration of void-whales.”

Godric—ruggedly handsome, salt-and-pepper, looking forty-five and always would—grinned at me with the warmth of a father figure who had no idea that his slightest twitch could shatter a galaxy. “The resistance. The big one. The one I’ve been letting simmer for, oh, five or six millennia. I excluded them from Gent’s passive annihilation so they could gather properly. Give themselves a fighting chance.” He paused, tilting his head as if listening to distant music. “Actually, they’ve been planning for nearly eight thousand years, if you count the initial thought that first triggered the weakness paradox. But the formal coalition only formed around four thousand years ago. Impressive dedication, really.”

I set the pastry down on the marble railing. My hand was steady, which surprised me. Inside, my soul was a trapped animal chewing on its own leg. “You’ve known about them the entire time. And you just… let them.”

“Of course. I have absolute omniscience, Tim. I’ve known since before the first of them was born. Every strategy meeting, every secret pact, every moment they believed they’d found a hidden flaw. It’s been quite entertaining, honestly.” He cracked his neck with a casual roll of his shoulders. “But they’re finally committed to an actual assault today, and I thought you’d want to watch. As my court wizard, it’s good to see what the opposition looks like, even if it’s all rather… academic.”

Academia, he called it. I wanted to laugh, but the sound that escaped my throat was more of a wheeze. I was Tim, high court wizard of Thronegate, direct descendant of Blake the Unbreakable, and I had spent my thirty-six years training to be the magical bulwark of the throne. And yet, standing next to Godric, I was less than a single mote of dust in a hurricane. That wasn’t self-pity; it was mathematical fact.

Godric gestured, and the Observatory’s dome irised open to reveal a viewing screen woven from compressed dimensional strata. It showed a sector of the omniverse that I didn’t recognize—a cold, grey region where reality foamed like disturbed water. And emerging from that foam was a fleet so vast, so overwhelmingly powerful, that my eyes failed to process it all at once.

There were not millions but trillions of vessels, each one a universe-compressed weapon-platform designed to burn through the fabric of metaphysics. Entire pantheons of gods from abandoned creation-myths flew in phalanxes, their divine forms augmented with technology stolen from the carcasses of dead multiverses. Entities made of pure narrative trope walked between the stars, their shapes flickering as they drew strength from every story ever told about triumph against impossible odds. And at the heart of the armada, four specific horrors pulsed with a potency that actually made my teeth ache.

The first was The Final Chronicler, a being whose form was a shifting library of endless books, each one the recorded death of a concept. It was said that to look upon the Chronicler was to see your own ending written in ink that had not yet dried. The second was the Omniversal Null-Lance, a weapon forged by a extinct species who had sought to destroy omnipotence itself, a lance of absolute nothing that could erase a target not just from existence but from every memory and record that ever acknowledged it. The third was the Legion of Infinite Regress, a military that existed in recursive nested realities—each soldier a iteration of the last, stronger and more deadly, their attacks arriving from timelines that folded outward like an infinite set of Russian dolls. And the fourth was a thing called the Conceptual Parasite, a sentient vacuum that fed on omnipotence; the more powerful its target, the more it grew, until the target was drained into a empty husk and the Parasite became the new apex.

Every one of these forces had been hyped by the omniversal rumor-mill for centuries. They were, by any reasonable standard, the most overpowered coalition ever assembled. And I could feel my own amplified magic—thousandfold daily since age twenty—screaming at me to run, to hide, to do anything but stand here.

Beside me, Godric stifled a yawn. “I’m going to let Gent handle it. I ordered him to hold back, though. I want to see if they can make him actually try for once.”

I turned to him. “Hold back… by how much, exactly?”

“He’s to suppress his power to a fraction represented by a decimal point, followed by an infinite recurrence of nines. So ninety-nine point nine nine nine—and so on, forever—percent of his total capacity. I was very explicit about the infinite recursion part. I want him to be virtually powerless against them.”

Virtually powerless. The phrase bounced around my skull like a ricocheting bullet. Gent, Godric’s sentient passive aura. I had seen Gent’s file in the royal archives: an autonomous defense mechanism that had evolved perfect adaptation, omniscience, omnipresence, and a disquieting loyalty complex. But the file also contained a footnote that had haunted me for years: Gent was essentially Godric’s metaphysical body heat. A waste byproduct. Infinitely weaker than the King himself. And Gent had siblings—countless others, more ferocious, more powerful, currently slumbering because Godric found their obsessive devotion irritating. The weakest of them all, and it was being told to fight at effectively zero percent strength.

The weight of what I was about to witness began to press on my shoulders like a collapsing star.

The attack commenced without warning—except Godric had known, and Gent had known, and the air in the room suddenly became heavier, as if a second atmosphere had slithered into existence. Gent was there. I couldn’t see it, not with my eyes, but my wizard-senses registered a presence that was everywhere and nowhere, a sentient field stretching across the entire sector with the patience of a spider.

The Final Chronicler opened its primary volume. The sound was a wet crack, like a spine breaking. Pages flipped, and a quill of liquid entropy dipped itself into an inkwell made from a dying universe. The Chronicler began to write, and the words were the shape of Godric’s doom. I know this because the Chronicler broadcast its intent as a psychic boast: it would author Godric’s final chapter, and reality would conform to the text.

Before the third syllable of that broadcast finished, the Chronicler’s book snapped shut. But not because it wanted to. Gent had decided that the book was a weapon of insolence, and so the book became a mouth. The covers warped into lips, the pages into teeth, and the entire volume folded inward and began to eat the Chronicler from the inside out. The being screamed—a sound that chopped my thoughts into static—as its own recorded deaths of countless concepts turned on it, each one a blade of narrativium that peeled its essence layer by layer. I saw the Chronicler’s conceptual form being flayed down to a single, agonized letter, and then Gent compressed that letter into a punctuation mark and struck it from existence. The tick of that erasure echoed across the void.

The Null-Lance fired. Its beam was not light but a propagating absence, a cone of absolute nothing that erased space, time, and probability as it advanced. The tech-priests who operated it had spent eons refining its calibration, ensuring it could target a being of omnipotent stature and remove him from the memory of the omniverse itself. The beam raced toward Godric, and my heart stopped.

And then Gent gently, almost lovingly, caught the beam. Not with a shield—with a single thought. The beam inverted. The nothing became a positive absence, a void that hungered not to erase but to fill. It flooded back along the beam’s original path, and when it touched the Null-Lance, the weapon didn’t explode; it blossomed. Every atom, every quark, every sub-dimensional thread of the weapon’s construction was transmuted into a needle of sharpened oblivion, and those needles rained upon the species that had built it. The Null-Smiths, a proud race of engineering demigods, were pierced by their own creation. Each needle delivered a payload of recursive agony: the victim experienced the sensation of being unmade, over and over, stretching into an infinite subjective duration compressed into a single Planck-second. Their collective scream was a data-burst of pure suffering that the viewing screen mercifully filtered, but I felt its echo in my soul.

The Legion of Infinite Regress attacked from every layer of reality at once. They were numberless and ever-multiplying, each soldier a stronger iteration, their weapons paradoxically perfected across nested timelines. Gent responded by creating a single, simple conceptual command: “Cease.” But it didn’t just stop the attackers. It traced their recursive existence back through every iterative layer, all the way to the original template, and it folded each layer into a single point of collapse. The soldiers—trillions of trillions—were crushed into a singularity of screaming flesh, a sphere of compressed agony that pulsed once, twice, and then evaporated. The silence that followed was the most profound I had ever experienced.

The Conceptual Parasite, sensing the display, lunged. It was a shambolic mass of hunger, a sentient law of consumption that fed on absolute power. It latched onto what it perceived as the source of the defense—Gent’s omnipotence—and opened its maw to drain it. But Gent was not omnipotent. Gent was a waste product. And the Parasite, in its arrogance, did not understand the difference. It bit into a power that was, by definition, the exhaust fume of something infinitely greater, and found itself starving. The more it tried to feed, the emptier it became, because the power it sought was not a thing that could be consumed; it was a secondary emission. The Parasite’s hunger looped back on itself, and Gent, with a flicker of what I could only interpret as sadistic satisfaction, trapped the Parasite in a state of perpetual self-cannibalism. I watched the creature devour its own limbs, its own core, its own concept, over and over, faster than light, until it was nothing but a faint, wailing echo. Gent then bottled that echo and tucked it away somewhere cold.

The rest of the armada—the pantheons, the trope-entities, the warships the size of galaxies—evaporated in the same instant. Gent didn’t even bother with individual methods; it simply enacted a simultaneous, omnipresent conclusion. Flesh unknitted from bone, mind from spirit, existence from being. The void became a churning slurry of disintegrating matter and fading screams. And then, as quickly as it began, it was over. The entire battle, from first chronicler’s ink to final whimper, had taken less than a second.

Godric’s viewing screen refreshed, showing only clean, empty space. Not a speck of debris, not a lingering flux of entropy. Gent had tidied up. I knew, from the archives, that the aura was fastidious because Godric disliked mess. And so it had scrubbed the scene until not a single molecule remained to testify that the most powerful coalition in history had ever even existed.

I turned to the King. My legs were not entirely reliable; I locked my knees to keep them from folding. Godric’s expression was one of mild, paternal disappointment—the face of a man who had watched a toddler proudly present a crayon drawing and then had to pretend it was a masterpiece. He clicked his tongue.

“They didn’t even make Gent try. I gave them every advantage. I let them gather. I forced Gent to hold back to a decimal of infinite nines. And still, nothing. I’m going to have to teach that aura actual restraint, because clearly I didn’t train it well enough.” He shook his head, sighing as if a beloved pet had failed to fetch a stick. “Gent, come here.”

The air beside him shimmered in a way that made my eyes water. I still couldn’t perceive Gent directly, but I felt it—a presence of vast, cold attention focusing entirely on its master. It practically vibrated with a desire to please, to be told it had done well. Godric reached out a hand and patted the empty space.

“Good boy, you cleaned up nicely. But you need to learn that when I say hold back ninety-nine point nine repeating percent, you should hold back so much that you almost fail. I want them to feel like they have a chance next time, okay? I’ll show you how it’s done. Restraint is a skill.” He said this with utter sincerity, the same Godric who had, on at least two occasions I knew of, accidentally obliterated universes because he’d been distracted imagining a new type of cheese. He always restored them instantly, yes, but the irony of *him* lecturing about restraint was so thick I could have choked on it.

I didn’t point this out. I valued my continued existence, if only barely.

And then Gent evolved.

I felt it before I saw it—a lurch in the metaphysical fabric, a sensation like reality briefly forgetting how to be real. The omniverse itself gave a tiny, submissive quiver, as if apologizing. I recalled the ancient texts: the Weakness Paradox had, by this era, collected every possible immunity and mastery, leaving creation unable to grant Godric meaningful new defenses. And so, in its shame, the omniverse compensated. It poured into Gent’s being a new layer of potency, a fresh expression of apocalyptic creativity, simply because it felt bad that it couldn't give the master anything useful.

Gent’s evolution manifested as a sound first—a low, harmonic thrum. Then the viewing screen flickered, and I saw the effect propagate across the omniverse: every being, everywhere, that had ever entertained the faintest notion of opposing Thronegate felt a sudden, sharp pain in their psyche. A brand, not of fire, but of absolute terror. Gent had woven itself into the fundamental narrative of existence, becoming the *teller* of the story of those who had died. It could not just kill; it could now author the posthumous cautionary tales, embedding the lived agony of its victims directly into the collective subconscious of every sentient race. It was a monument of pain, a library of screams, a broadcast that would echo for eternity. Anyone who thought of rebellion would not just fear death; they would live the death of the Legion, the Chronicler, the Parasite, over and over, a pre-play of their own annihilation.

I watched, my mind fracturing at the edges, as Godric’s face split into a wide, delighted grin. “Oh, look at that!” he said, and his voice was that of a man whose dog had just performed a new trick. “Little Gent learned to write stories! That’s adorable. I didn’t even teach him that. He’s such a clever boy.”

He turned to me, his eyes crinkling with genuine paternal affection for his passive aura. “Isn’t he clever, Tim? It’s like watching a puppy speak its first words.”

I opened my mouth. No sound came out. Because inside my head, a new and worse understanding was dawning.

Godric’s amplification had never stopped. I’d known this intellectually, but standing in the Observatory, the weight of that mathematical horror crashed into me with the force of a collapsing cosmos. His power did not grow by linear increments. It multiplied by itself every Planck second—no, not even a simple multiplication anymore. The Weakness Paradox had evolved that rate beyond the scope of infinite mathematics. The man standing next to me, grinning at his “puppy,” was, in this exact moment, a being that could effortlessly annihilate infinite copies of his own self from a single Planck second ago. The Godric of *now* could erase the Godric of *just then*, and the gap was so vast that the earlier version wouldn’t even be able to conceive of the attack. And the next Planck second, the gap would widen again. And again. Forever.

The coalition I had just witnessed obliterated? They had spent millennia preparing. But every day, every hour, every infinitesimal fraction of a second they spent planning, the target of their hatred had been accelerating away from them at a rate that literally had no ceiling. They had not merely lost. They had been racing against a finish line that folded space-time to stay ahead. Their defeat was sealed at the very moment the first of them had formed the thought of opposition, because that thought had triggered the paradox and fed the engine. The battle—if you could call it that—was just the playback.

I thought of my own power, the court wizard amplification that made me a god by mortal standards. It was nothing. A single drop against an infinite ocean. My entire existence was only possible because Godric allowed it, and even that allowance was not a right but a whim.

The air in the Observatory felt thick, oppressive. My lungs worked but I couldn’t get enough breath. My heart pounded a rhythm that seemed to ask: *why do you keep beating? what’s the point?*

Godric stretched, his muscles shifting under the plain tunic as if he’d just finished a light jog. “Well, that was a nice diversion. I think I’ll have that pastry now, if it’s still fresh. And then maybe we can discuss the next diplomatic summit. Blake’s great-grandson told me the delegates from the new subjugated zone are nervous; I might do another prank to lighten the mood. Blake himself would have loved that.”

I stared at him. The man who had just let his body heat slaughter the ultimate resistance. The king who was genuinely kind to his people, who protected women with absolute ferocity, who would personally comfort a crying child and then, in the same hour, unmake a rebellious cosmos without a flicker of remorse. He was, simultaneously, the best father the omniverse had ever known and the most horrifying predator existence could produce.

And I loved him. I did. He was my king, my friend, the man who had ruffled my hair when I was a boy and told me I’d make a fine wizard. But that love lived inside a chest that also held a cold, yawning void of existential dread, and the two things clung to each other like twins sharing a single grave.

Godric picked up my abandoned pastry from the railing and took an experimental bite. “Still good. Come, Tim. Tell me what you thought of the Legion’s formation—was it a bit too predictable? I could have given them advice, but that would have spoiled the game.”

I followed him out of the Observatory on legs that felt like borrowed bones. The echo of Gent’s new, terrifying power hummed in the background of reality, a constant broadcast of agony. And Godric was already planning his next joke.

The omniverse stretched before us, quiet and orderly, entirely in the palm of a man who didn’t even have to try. And I walked behind him, a court wizard with a thousandfold daily amplification, and felt as small and fragile as a candle flame in the heart of a sun.

--- End of draft ---
