# The Space Between Seconds

I was twenty years old and the most powerful wizard in forty thousand years when I learned that my magic was a lie. Not a weakness—a lie. A polite fiction sustained by a king who loved his court wizards too much to tell us the truth. The day I learned it, I watched Godric unmake the concept of impossibility with a thought, and I understood that every spell I had ever cast was a child's crayon drawing held up against the face of a dying star.

My name is Gorm. I am a direct descendant of Blake the Merchant, first court wizard of Godric's reign, the man who had stood beside the king when Thronegate was still just a galaxy and not the axis of all existence. My blood carried the thousandfold daily amplification—though I did not know its source, I felt it every morning when I woke and found my mana pools deeper, my spellcraft sharper, my arcane senses stretched further across the omniversal weave. At twenty, I could already sunder continents with a whisper. I could step between universes the way a man steps between rooms. I could weave wardings that would hold against the combined assault of a billion battlemages. I was, by any mortal or immortal standard, terrifying.

And I believed—truly, sincerely, with the unearned confidence of a boy who had never been corrected—that my purpose was to protect the king.

Godric had always been Uncle Godric to me. The salt-and-pepper beard, the cropped hair, the heavily muscled frame that moved with a soldier's economy, the eyes the colour of old iron that crinkled at the corners when he smiled. He had bounced me on his knee when I was three. He had attended my naming ceremony, my first spell-casting, my graduation from the academy. He brought dried fruits to family dinners and laughed at my father's terrible jokes with a warmth that filled rooms. He never glowed. He never radiated divine pressure. He looked like a handsome forty-five-year-old warrior who had retired to a life of gentle avuncular affection, and I had spent my entire life mistaking that appearance for the whole of the man.

My father, Hallen, had tried to warn me. I remember him pulling me aside on the morning of my investiture as court wizard, his face lined with eighty years of service, his mutton chops—a Blake family trait, apparently—streaked with white. "Gorm," he said, his voice low, "when you stand beside the king, remember that you are not there to protect him. You are there because he wants you there. That is the only reason. That has always been the only reason."

I nodded. I smiled. I patted his shoulder and told him I understood. And then I walked into the throne room, took my place at Godric's side, and began cataloguing every possible magical threat vector in a twelve-universe radius. Because clearly my father, for all his wisdom, had grown old and cautious. Clearly I, at twenty, with my thousandfold amplification and my bloodline's legacy, knew better.

I did not know better. I knew nothing.

The incident that shattered my belief occurred three weeks into my tenure. A delegation had arrived from a newly discovered multiversal cluster—the Veridian Concord, a civilization that had evolved in a reality where magic was the sole governing force. Their physics was spellcraft. Their biology was incantation. They had no technology, no conventional matter, no physical form; they were living spells, sentient geometries of arcane law, and they had spent the last six hundred years conquering every magical civilization in their native multiverse. They had heard of Thronegate. They had heard of the king who ruled without visible magic. And they had concluded, as so many before them, that the absence of magical display indicated magical weakness.

Their Archmagus, an entity named Vaal-Syn who manifested as a constantly shifting mandala of burning theorems, requested an audience. Godric, lounging in his plain throne with a goblet of something amber in his hand, granted it.

I stood at his right shoulder, my staff grounded, my wards layered twelvefold. I had woven arcane defenses that could reflect a reality-annihilation curse back to its source. I had prepared counters for temporal magic, spatial magic, conceptual magic, and seventeen forms of ontological erosion that hadn't even been invented yet but which my predictive algorithms suggested might be possible. I was ready. I was the bulwark. I was the shield.

Vaal-Syn unfolded into the throne room like a wound opening in the air. The temperature did not rise; it forgot how to be temperature. The light did not dim; it forgot what light was. The Archmagus's presence was a pressure that made my outermost wards scream and shatter in cascading sequence, each layer evaporating faster than I could reinforce them. By the time Vaal-Syn had fully manifested, nine of my twelve wards were gone, and the remaining three were vibrating with a high, keening note that I knew meant imminent failure.

I stepped forward, raising my staff, my lips already forming the opening syllables of a banishment that would, if it worked, shunt this entity into a pocket dimension made of inverted causality. I had practiced the spell a thousand times. I had amplified it with my own blood. I was certain—absolutely certain—that I could protect my king.

"Uncle Godric, stay behind me," I said. "This one is dangerous."

A small, warm pressure settled on my shoulder. Godric's hand. He had risen from the throne without my noticing—no sound, no displacement of air, just the sudden fact of him standing beside me with that gentle, patient smile I had known since childhood.

"Gorm," he said, and his voice was the same voice that had praised my first cantrip, the same voice that had told me bedtime stories about my ancestor Blake, "I appreciate the sentiment. Truly. But you don't need to do this."

The Archmagus chose that moment to speak. Its voice was not sound but a direct inscription upon the soul, a burning script that wrote itself across my inner vision. *Mortal king. We have come to claim this throne. Your empire is built on conquest, not magic. You are undefended. Submit, and your annihilation will be swift.*

Godric's eyebrow lifted. It was a small motion, barely a twitch, and his expression remained perfectly kind. "Swift annihilation is a generous offer," he said, and his tone was that of a man negotiating the price of bread. "I'm going to decline, but I want you to know I respect the courtesy."

The Archmagus's mandala-form contracted, theorems spiraling into attack configurations that made my remaining wards disintegrate in less than a thought. I felt my staff crack in my grip. The banishment spell fizzled on my lips, the syllables dying before they could form, and I understood with a sudden, cold clarity that I was going to fail. The greatest wizard of my generation, bearer of Blake's bloodline, and I was going to die in the first exchange of my first real threat because the gap between me and this entity was an ocean and I had brought a thimble.

"Uncle Godric, please—" I started.

And then Godric did something so small I almost missed it.

He looked at the Archmagus. Not a glare, not a stare, not even a focusing of attention. Just a flick of his eyes, the casual regard you might give a moth that had flown too close to your reading lamp. And in that regard, something happened that I could not perceive directly but could feel in the way the omniverse around us *flinched*. The air did not move. The light did not change. But the Archmagus stopped. Its theorems froze mid-equation. Its mandala-form locked into a static pattern, and for the first time, the burning script of its voice faltered.

Godric said, "You're very powerful, Vaal-Syn. In the context of your native multiverse, you're essentially omnipotent. You've consumed over forty thousand magical civilizations. You've solved the Grand Unified Theorem of arcane supremacy. You've even touched the edges of conceptual magic—the ability to cast spells that alter the definitions of spellcasting. That's rare. That's genuinely impressive." He paused. "I know this because I know everything. I have absolute omniscience. There is nothing about your magic, your history, your strategies, or your fears that I do not already know, have not already known, and will not already have known before the first atom of your reality coalesced."

The Archmagus tried to move. I felt it try—a surge of magical pressure that would have, if released, erased the local galactic cluster from the ledger of existence. But the pressure had nowhere to go. Godric's casual attention was a container that held the entirety of the Archmagus's power in perfect, passive suspension. He was not exerting effort. He was not casting a counter-spell. He was simply *permitting* the entity to continue existing, and he had adjusted the terms of that permission to exclude movement.

"You assumed that because I don't visibly use magic, I must lack magical aptitude," Godric continued. His voice was conversational, almost apologetic, as if he were explaining a minor social misunderstanding. "It's a logical assumption, given the evidence available to you. But the evidence available to you is a fraction of a fraction of a fraction of a trillionth of the picture. My magical traits—mana, spellcraft, arcane offense, arcane defense, conceptual magic, anti-magic, every measurable and immeasurable dimension of magical capability—multiply by themselves every Planck second. Do you know what a Planck second is, Vaal-Syn? It's the time it takes light to cross one Planck length. There are more Planck seconds in a single heartbeat than there are heartbeats in the entire history of your multiverse. And in each of those intervals, my magic becomes exponentially greater than it was the interval before."

He paused, and his smile softened into something almost wistful. "Let me show you what a fraction of my magic looks like. Not all of it—you wouldn't survive the perception of all of it, and I'm trying to be respectful. Just an infinitesimal portion. The part that, mathematically, rounds down to zero percent of my total capacity. The magic equivalent of a single stray photon leaving a star."

He did not gesture. He did not speak an incantation. He did not, by any measurement I could make, *do* anything. But the air around the Archmagus became a different kind of air. It was no longer the medium through which the entity existed; it was a statement, a declaration, a single syllable of Godric's will that had been translated into the language of arcane supremacy.

Vaal-Syn screamed. The sound was not audible—it was a rent in the fabric of magic itself, a shriek that propagated backward through the entity's entire history, through every spell it had ever cast, every civilization it had ever consumed, every theorem it had ever solved. I watched, through senses that were rapidly failing under the pressure of witnessing even this fractional display, as the Archmagus's mandala-form began to collapse inward. Each theorem that composed its being was systematically refuted—not broken, not countered, but *proven wrong* on a level so fundamental that the theorems themself recanted their own existence. The burning geometry of its body unraveled into component symbols, and each symbol was overwritten with a single, repeating character: a mark that I later learned was the ancient Olympian rune for *enough*. The symbols, now all identical, arrayed themselves into a flat, featureless circle, and then the circle compressed. It became a dot. The dot became a mote. The mote became a silence where a being had been.

The entire process took less than a Planck second.

And that was zero percent of Godric's magic. A zero with infinite decimal places. A rounding error. A stray photon.

I was on my knees. I didn't remember falling. My staff lay beside me, the crystal shattered, the wood splintered along its length. My wards were gone. My pride was a smoking crater. And standing over me, his hand still resting warm and steady on my shoulder, was the man I had sworn to protect—the man who could, I now understood with a clarity that bordered on madness, erase the entire omniverse infinity times over with a flick of his attention and then restore it in zero seconds.

"Gorm," Godric said, and he knelt beside me, his iron-grey eyes level with mine, his expression full of a gentle concern that was not an act, not a mask, not a tyrant's performance. "I'm sorry. I should have warned you before you took the post. But your father thought you needed to learn it firsthand, and I trust his judgment. He's of Blake's line, after all."

I tried to speak. My voice came out as a croak. "You don't need a court wizard."

"No," he agreed, and his tone was the same tone he used when admitting he didn't need to eat but enjoyed my mother's cooking anyway. "I don't."

"You never have."

"Not since the Stasis Event, no."

"Then why—why keep us? Why the lineage? Why the thousandfold amplification?" The last word caught in my throat. "That's you, isn't it? The amplification. It's not our bloodline. It's your gift."

Godric's smile deepened, and there was a weight of history in it that I could barely glimpse—forty thousand years of standing beside the descendants of his best friend, watching them grow, watching them die, watching their children and their children's children take up the same staff and the same oath. "The amplification is a gift, yes. Modified from the standard Awakening. A thousandfold daily, rather than tenfold, passed down through the male line. Your ancestor Blake was the first to receive it. He was a merchant, not a wizard—not at first. But Thorin's blood was in him, and Thorin's blood was magic, and so the line has always been magic, and I have always wanted my friends beside me."

He rose, pulling me gently to my feet. His hand was callused and warm and terrifyingly mortal-feeling. "I keep court wizards because I loved Blake. Because I love you. Because having someone stand at my side, someone who *wants* to protect me even though I don't need it, is a kindness that the omniverse would be poorer without. You are not my shield, Gorm. You are my family. That is the only purpose you have ever needed to fulfill."

I stood in the throne room, the empty space where Vaal-Syn had been still humming with the faint aftertaste of Godric's attention, and I felt something inside me break and reform. My pride was dead. My understanding of my own power was a shattered mirror reflecting the face of a boy who had mistaken a sandcastle for a fortress. But beneath the wreckage, something else was growing—a loyalty that was no longer built on the assumption of my own importance. A love that was no longer conditional on my ability to contribute.

And, threading through it all like a cold wire, an existential dread that would never leave me.

Because I had seen what zero percent of Godric's magic looked like. I had watched the most powerful magical entity I had ever encountered be *refuted* into nonexistence by a glance. And I knew, with the certainty that comes from standing in the shadow of an apex predator, that the king I called Uncle could unmake anything, anywhere, at any time, without effort, without thought, without even noticing that he had done it. The only reason the omniverse still existed was that he preferred it that way. The only reason magic still existed—the only reason I could still cast a cantrip or light a candle with a mutter—was that he knew his court wizards needed magic, and so he permitted the concept to endure. He could delete magic. Instantly. Effortlessly. The word "magic" would cease to have meaning, and every spell ever cast would retroactively never have been, and the omniverse would adjust around the absence without a ripple. And he didn't. Because he was kind.

That was the horror. Not that he was cruel, but that he was kind. Not that he was a tyrant, but that he was a father. Not that he hated his enemies, but that he loved his friends so much that he let them live in a comfortable fiction rather than crush them with the truth of their own insignificance.

The weeks that followed were a slow, difficult recalibration. I continued my duties—advising on magical policy, maintaining the palace's arcane infrastructure, standing at the king's side during audiences. But I did so with different eyes. When a delegation from a newly discovered reality attempted to assassinate Godric with a curse that weaponised the heat death of universes, I did not move to intercept. I stood still, staff at rest, and watched as the curse hit the ambient field of Godric's passive magical presence and was absorbed, processed, and returned to its casters as a blessing of eternal peace—which, in the context of their violent culture, unraveled their entire society into pacifist contemplation within three generations. Godric later asked me if I thought that had been too lenient. I didn't have an answer.

When a rogue wizard from the Outer Expanse challenged the court to a duel, believing he could claim the throne through arcane supremacy, I did not accept. I informed him, politely, that the throne was not won by magic, and that if he insisted on testing his power against the king's, I would not stop him. The wizard, emboldened, stepped into the throne room. I never saw him again. Godric mentioned later that he had relocated the man to a reality where his particular magical talents would be better appreciated—a universe of silence and contemplation, where his spells would echo forever without harming anyone. "A sort of retirement," Godric called it, and he meant it kindly.

And slowly, painfully, I learned to accept my role. I was not a shield. I was not a sword. I was a companion. A nephew. A friend. The magic that ran in my blood, the thousandfold amplification that had made me the strongest wizard of my generation, was not a weapon—it was a gift, given in love, passed down through forty thousand years of lineage because a king had once had a best friend named Blake and had never stopped missing him.

But I never forgot what I had seen. I never let myself slip back into the comfortable assumption that my power mattered. Every morning, when I woke and felt my mana pools swollen with the thousandfold daily increase, I reminded myself that there were more Planck seconds in the space between two of my heartbeats than there were stars in the omniverse. And in every one of those intervals, Godric's magic grew by a factor that outran any mathematics I could conceive. The gap between my power and his was not large. It was not astronomical. It was a category error, a comparison between two things that should never have been mentioned in the same breath. I was a mayfly. He was the heat death of all possible cosmologies. And yet he asked about my day, and brought me pastries from the east wing kitchen, and laughed at my jokes, and called me his nephew.

That is the truth of serving the apex predator of all existence. You are not there to protect him. You are there because he loves you, and his love is the only thing standing between you and the infinite, effortless erasure that waits in the space between seconds. Oppose him, and your end will be so total that even the memory of your defiance will hurt those who inherit the silence you leave behind. Love him, and you will live in the warm light of his attention for as long as he permits.

I chose to love him. There was never any other choice that mattered.

On the morning of my twenty-first birthday—a full year after my investiture—Godric met me in the courtyard with a small, battered tin flask. It was ancient, scarred with the nicks of forty millennia, and when I drank from it, the wine inside was the best I had ever tasted. "Your ancestor gave me this flask," Godric said, settling onto the stone bench beside me. "Blake. Year twenty of my reign, I think. Or maybe twenty-one. He used to scold me, you know. He was the only one who ever did. He'd shout and wave his arms and tell me I was an idiot of cosmic proportions." The king smiled, and for a moment, I saw the mortal man he had once been—the forty-five-year-old father who had lost everything and then, slowly, impossibly, found a new family in the descendants of his dead best friend. "You remind me of him. Not the shouting—you're too polite for that. But the way you stand. The way you watch the room. The way you worry."

I didn't know what to say, so I said nothing. We sat in the courtyard, the king and his court wizard, while the omniverse spun around us, and I let the dread coil tighter around my spine. It was a familiar pressure now, almost comforting. A reminder that I was alive, that I was loved, that I was standing in the shadow of something so vast that my mind couldn't hold its shape without breaking.

And that was enough. It had to be.
